description: This optical micrograph displays the microstructure of an Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel containing 4.6 wt.% Al after aging at 600°C for 16 h, with a scale bar of 20 μm. The image reveals polygonal austenite grains bounded by a prominent continuous or semi-continuous dark phase network along the grain boundaries, accompanied by finer intragranular particles and occasional plate-like precipitates within the matrix. The figure caption states this is κ-phase precipitation, whereas the accompanying paper text reports that for low aluminum content the carbide phase precipitates during aging as a network along grain boundaries (Fig. 2), which is highly detrimental to impact toughness. This micrograph thus provides direct metallographic evidence of the embrittling grain-boundary precipitation regime that must be excluded when establishing composition limits for structural steels. It supports the central scientific conclusion that aluminum contents below approximately 6.2 wt.% promote unacceptable grain-boundary carbide networks upon aging, in contrast to the homogeneous precipitation field sought at higher aluminum concentrations.
